coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient degradation of dyes by carbon quantum dots/N-doped zinc oxide (CQD/N-ZnO) photocatalyst and its compatibility on three different commercial dyes under daylight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Muthulingam, S.
Lee, In-Hwan
Uthirakumar, Periyayya
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly carbon quantum dots/nitrogen-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) composites were successfully prepared by a facile one-step method. The various techniques were employed to characterize the phase structure, morphology and optical properties of CQD/N-ZnO composites. The nitrogen doping was confirmed by Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Three commercial dyes, such as malachite green, methylene blue and fluorescein dyes were chosen to investigate the photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO under daylight irradiation. It was found that the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst established a high compatibility to degrade all three commercial dyes within 30–45min, under daylight irradiation. Also, it remains capable of reusing the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst for repeated photocatalytic performance due to anti-photocorrosion offered by CQDs. The synergetic effect of N-doping and CQDs is key to design a new class of photocatalyst for environmental remediation under naturally available daylight source.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide
Dyes
Photocatalyst
Degradation
Daylight

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confirmation of N-doping by XPS analysis
Proposed mechanism for superior photocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites. The Raman spectrum (b) and the high resolution core level (c) N 1s (d) Zn 2p, and (e) O 1s XPS spectrum of CQD/N-ZnO composites. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites.
FESEM images of (a) N-doped ZnO and (b) CQD/N-ZnO composites. TEM image of (c and d) CQD/N-ZnO composites [Insets: HRTEM images with the lattice space values of carbon and ZnO].
Schematic demonstrates the experimental setup and mechanistic pathway of CQD/N-ZnO on commercial dyes under naturally available daylight irradiation.

The photocatalytic activity of (a) N-doped ZnO, (b) CQD/N-ZnO, and (c) the comparative profile on MG dye solution under daylight irradiation. (d) The photographs of MG dye solution taken after the regular intervals of 0, 15, 30, 45, and 60min of daylight irradiation in presence of CQD/N-ZnO (top) and N-doped ZnO (bottom).

The photocatalytic activity of (a) N-doped ZnO, (b) CQD/N-ZnO, and (c) the comparative profile on MB dye solution under daylight irradiation. (d) The photographs of MB dye solution taken after the regular intervals of 0, 15, 30, 45, and 60min of daylight irradiation in presence of CQD/N-ZnO (top) and N-doped ZnO (bottom).

The photocatalytic activity of (a) N-doped ZnO, (b) CQD/N-ZnO, and (c) the comparative profile on FL dye solution under daylight irradiation. (d) The photographs of FL dye solution taken after the regular intervals of 0, 15, 30, 45, and 60min of daylight irradiation in presence of CQD/N-ZnO (top) and N-doped ZnO (bottom).
Comparative photocatalytic degradation of MG, MB and FL dyes upon different photocatalysts on 4th successive cycle under daylight irradiation.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient degradation of dyes by carbon quantum dots/N-doped zinc oxide (CQD/N-ZnO) photocatalyst and its compatibility on three different commercial dyes under daylight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED], Department of Chemistry, Sona College of Technology, Salem, Tamil Nadu 636 005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED]

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Advanced Materials Engineering and Research Center of Advanced Materials Development, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea (P. Uthirakumar).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly carbon quantum dots/nitrogen-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) composites were successfully prepared by a facile one-step method. The various techniques were employed to characterize the phase structure, morphology and optical properties of CQD/N-ZnO composites. The nitrogen doping was confirmed by Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Three commercial dyes, such as malachite green, methylene blue and fluorescein dyes were chosen to investigate the photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO under daylight irradiation. It was found that the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst established a high compatibility to degrade all three commercial dyes within 30–45min, under daylight irradiation. Also, it remains capable of reusing the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst for repeated photocatalytic performance due to anti-photocorrosion offered by CQDs. The synergetic effect of N-doping and CQDs is key to design a new class of photocatalyst for environmental remediation under naturally available daylight source.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic semiconductors are suggested to use as a photocatalyst to remove the major organic contaminants originated from the textile and food industries [1]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) is one of the promising photocatalysts as of being its high catalytic activity, low cost, wide band gap (3.37eV) and environmental friendliness [2]. A quick recombination and the narrow absorption band of the ZnO photocatalyst are the major anxieties that affect their photocatalytic efficiency. Hence, a suitable alteration is fairly required for developing new class of ZnO nanostructures by incorporating either secondary semiconductor metal [3] or carbon nanoparticle [4–10].

In the recent days, carbon based materials have received the considerable attention owing to their advantages in non-toxic, photostability, low cost, heavy metal-free and eco-friendly in nature. Although, several works were published comprising C-60, graphene and carbon nanofiber as electron scavenging agents [4,7,8], it was rare work found with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) related photocatalysts [9]. Moreover, the CQDs itself received the significant advantages over other carbon based structures due to their higher photo-induced electron transfer [8] and up-conversion properties [6]. Besides, the CQDs will offer the higher tendency to shield the ZnO crystals from the photocorrosion. Thus, the coverage of CQD on the ZnO crystals will be an ideal tactic for endorsing the improvement of charge separation to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency along with the hindered photocorrosion.

Till now, most of the recent photocatalytic investigations are established based on the usage of UV-lamp or visible light driven photocatalysis rather than the naturally available daylight [2,4–10]. In general, it is inevitable that at least a specific light source apparently essential to initiate the electron–hole pair separation leading to the subsequent formation of active species for photodegradation. As a consequence, it becomes an expensive technique by offering such a specific light source during commercialization of these process in industries, especially for treating large quantity of wastewater. Therefore, it is obvious that a good photocatalyst should be of inexpensive, non-toxic to the environment and it should be capable of operating under a cheaper or affordable light source. Hence, utilizing naturally available daylight source will be a better alternative way to remove the dye molecules in huge volume in industry. However, limited reports are found involved with the daylight irradiation [11].

In addition, the maximum of the recent reports are mainly focused on any one of the selected dyes, ranging from methylene blue (MB), methyl orange, rhodamine B, and so on [4–10] and rare work involved for comparing the photocatalytic performance with more than two different dyes with a single photocatalyst [12]. To the extent of our knowledge, the photocatalytic investigation on three commercial dyes utilizing naturally available daylight source at an ambient condition has not been reported yet. Thus, this article reports a rapid photocatalytic activity and compatibility of carbon quantum dots/nitrogen-doped ZnO (CQD/N-ZnO) photocatalyst on three different commercial dyes under the naturally available daylight irradiation.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. For synthesizing nitrogen doped ZnO (N-doped ZnO), about 1.25g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 125mL of double distilled water (DDW). Ammonia solution (18%) was added drop-wise to keep the solution pH to be around 10. The resultant clear solution was filtered and it was further diluted with 50mL of DDW and kept at 60°C on a constant temperature bath for 26h. White crystals of ZnO were collected, washed with distilled water and ethanol several times and dried [13].

A facile one-step wet chemical method was used to synthesize CQDs with a slight modification of Hummer’s method [14]. Typically, 50mg of carbon black pigment (HIBLACK Orion) was put into the mixture of 13mL H2SO4 and 6.5mL HNO3 at room temperature and sonicated for few minutes for better dispersion. The reaction was maintained at 240°C for 2h and it was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then, the reaction mixture was neutralized with sodium hydroxide (1M) solution at room temperature and excess salts were removed by repeated dialysis for 2days. The average particle size of CQD was measured to be ∼2.5nm.

For CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst, a required amount (10μL) of CQD solution was mixed with 50mg of N-doped ZnO crystals dispersed in 25mL of ethanol and stirred for 30min at room temperature. Filtered and washed with ethanol several times and dried.

5mg of photocatalyst was suspended in 100mL of malachite green (MG) aqueous solution (1.0×10−4 M) taken in a glass beaker and agitated in the absence of light for 30min to establish an absorption–desorption equilibrium. After a given daylight exposure time, about 5mL of the mixture was withdrawn and the catalysts were separated by centrifuging of suspension in prior to measure the photodegradation. Photocatalytic measurements were performed under naturally available daylight source. The daylight intensity (33×103 ±5000lx) was measured by digital lux-meter during Oct to Dec 2014 (11am to 2pm). All the experiments were undertaken in triplicate with errors below 4% and average values were reported. The similar experiments were carried out for MB dye and fluorescein (FL) dyes to investigate the degradation trend under daylight irradiation.

The powder X-ray diffractograms (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded by a Rigaku using Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.1546nm) monochromated by a nickel filter. Morphology was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SUPRA-40VP equipped with an EDXS system) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2010). The surface electronic states of the photocatalyst were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos. Inc), Monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV, 150W). Raman spectra were collected on Raman spectroscope (nano-Raman, NTEGRA) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. Photocatalytic activity was analyzed with an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu).

Fig. 1 a shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of CQD/N-ZnO and N-doped ZnO to check the crystal structure and phase composition. It is clear that all of the diffraction peaks are perfectly indexed as hexagonal phase ZnO (JCPDS No. 79-0206). The characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ values of 31.7°, 33.4° and 36.2°, representing (100), (002) and (101) plane, assigned to the crystal planes of ZnO [12]. Whereas, notably, a broader diffraction peak (star mark) was observed at 26° related to the graphitic (002) peak. In addition, a slight shifting of (002) diffraction peak from 36.21° to 36.08°, was evidently seen in Fig. S1, indicating that the CQDs were successfully covered on the ZnO crystals [5]. This result confirms the existence of CQD and ZnO phases in CQD/N-ZnO composite and it was further confirmed by Raman spectra investigation.

Fig. 1b shows the Raman spectrum of CQD/N-ZnO composite. The occurrence of Raman-active modes of ZnO at 331cm−1, 434cm−1, and 580cm−1 are associated to the second-order scattering arising from zone-boundary phonons 2E2, E2 (high) and the polar symmetry modes A1 (LO) [15], respectively. Similarly, carbon related Raman-active modes of graphitic (G) and defect (D) bands were observed at 1340cm−1 and 1600cm−1, respectively, suggesting the existence of CQD and ZnO related Raman active modes in CQD/N-ZnO composite. Thus, the Raman results provide an additional support that the CQDs were successfully covered on ZnO crystals, which is in good agreement with the XRD results. However, no evidence assigned to N-doping was detected in XRD and Raman analysis. Hence, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to assess the chemical state of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of N-doping by XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(c)–(e) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of core level N 1s, Zn 2p, and O 1s of CQD/N-ZnO composites. The full survey spectrum in Fig. S2 demonstrates that the occurrence of main elements such as Zn, O, C and N I in the CQD/N-ZnO composites. The core level high resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) shows that peaks appeared at 396.1eV, 397.2eV, 398.8eV, and 400.1eV, which indicates the nitrogen doping onto the ZnO crystals. Especially, a high intense peak appeared at 396.1eV is assigned to –N–Zn–N– bond and the lesser intense peak at 397.2eV may be assigned to –N–C– bond [16]. The other two peaks at 398.8eV and 400.1eV can be assigned to –N–H– bond and –O–Zn–N–, respectively [16,17]. These results were strongly authenticate that the successful doping of nitrogen in ZnO crystals. Similarly, the binding energy values related to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 were observed at 1017eV and 1041eV, respectively. Also, the deconvolution of O 1s XPS spectrum exhibits a main peak with a binding energy of 527.2eV representing to the –O–Zn–O– bond and the other higher binding energy values at 528.3eV and 529.6eV are corresponding to the oxygen defects within the ZnO materials [16].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composite were recorded, as shown in Fig. 1f. The characteristic absorption peak with a band-gap transition in the UV region was observed in both samples. However, CQD/N-ZnO composite shows a broad absorption extended up to 600nm when compared to the N-doped ZnO. Extrapolation of tangent line through the point of inflexion in the absorption band and the photon energy axis gives the band gap energy for N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites (Fig. S3). The band gap value of N-doped ZnO was observed to be 3.21eV, while, a slightly lower value detected for CQD/N-ZnO at 3.10eV, indicating that the wider absorption capability of CQD/N-ZnO composite. Hence, it can be recommended to use as an effective photocatalyst even in the visible region.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM and HRTEM images of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites. The average particle size of N-doped ZnO crystal was found to be ∼25nm. The particle sizes are remain same and there was no substantial changes in the size was observed even after CQD coverage. However, the elemental composition results in Fig. S4 reveals the existence of zinc, oxygen, carbon and nitrogen that strongly confirms the presence of both phases of CQD and ZnO in CQD/N-ZnO composites. Fig. 2(c) and (d) shows the TEM and HRTEM images of CQD/N-ZnO composites. Insets in Fig. 2(d) display the HRTEM images of CQD/N-ZnO composites and the lattice spacing were measured to be ∼0.26nm and ∼0.32nm, respectively for ZnO and carbon [5]. The above results concluded that the coverage of CQDs on the ZnO crystals.

Fig. 3 illustrates the schematic to demonstrate the photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO on commercial dyes under naturally available daylight source. Here, the photocatalytic experiments were conducted at the open terrace in such a way to render the naturally available direct daylight source for photocatalytic reaction. A magnetic stirrer was used to mix the aqueous dye solution at ambient condition. The samples were collected regularly to monitor the degradation trend after removal of photocatalyst. The photocatalytic activity of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO composites on three different commercial dyes, such as MG, MB and FL dyes were evaluated under naturally available daylight source.

Meanwhile the MG dye was largely used to color the silk, wool and leather industry, it is inevitable to stop entering into the environment from municipal wastewater and it is suspected to be capable of causing gene damage and causing cancer [18,19]. Fig. 4 shows the photodegradation of MG dye in presence of N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts under daylight irradiation. A superior photodegradation trend was observed (Fig 4(a) and (b)). For instance, after 30min of daylight irradiation, the degradation efficiency of 60% and 100% was calculated for N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst, respectively (Table S5). On the other hand, around 18% of MG dye degradation was noticed after 60min of daylight irradiation without catalyst (Fig. S6). This observation was further supported with the photographs of aqueous MG dye solution taken after 0, 15, 30, 45 and 60min of daylight irradiation, indicating the complete removal of MG dye. Beside, a gradual decrease in color was observed in case of N-doped ZnO. Conversely, a similar type of photocatalyst, ZnO–rGO composite needs ∼90min of UV irradiation to degrade ∼85% of MG dye [20]. Based on this result, it was concluded that a superior photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst under daylight and it required just 30min to degrade entire MG dye.

Likewise, the photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO composites was further validated with MB dye under daylight irradiation. In general, the MB dyes are used for dyeing of silk, leather, plastics, paper, cotton, and also in the manufacture of paints and printing inks [21]. It creates plenty of health hazards upon inhalation, mainly it may cause hypertension, precordial pain, dizziness, mental confusion, and hemolytic anemia [22]. Fig. 5 shows the photocatalytic activity of CQD/N-ZnO and N-doped ZnO photocatalysts on the degradation of MB dye under daylight, together with the photographs. A gradual decrease in the absorption peak of MB dye was observed in case of N-doped ZnO, while in Fig 5(b), a quick decline was noticed in CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst. For example, the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst required ∼45min to degrade MB dye completely, but N-doped ZnO needed ∼60min to degrade 82% of MB dye under daylight. It was further substantiated with the photographs of aqueous MB dye solution taken after regular intervals, as shown in Fig. 5d. Besides, pure MB dye itself degrades to the extent of ∼15% (in 60min) under daylight irradiation without catalyst (Fig. S6). The impact of superior performance of CQD/N-ZnO was compared with other recent reports related to the CQD covered metal oxide photocatalysts. For instance, CQD/α-Fe2O3 photocatalyst destroys MB dye in 90min in presence of H2O2 under visible light [23]. Also, ZnO/graphene photocatalyst shows ∼90% degradation of MB dye in 40min under UV light [24] and TiO2/C-dots needs at least 4h to degrade MB dye under visible light [16].

Furthermore, yet another commercial FL dye was chosen to check the compatibility of CQD/N-ZnO and N-doped ZnO photocatalysts under daylight irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6 . Here, CQD/N-ZnO exhibited an excellent FL dye degradation (∼95%) was noticed within 15min of daylight, while 75% for N-doped ZnO photocatalyst. More specifically, CQD/N-ZnO needed ∼30min to degrade entire FL dye and N-doped ZnO required 60min to degrade 92% of dye, as shown in Fig 6c. Besides, in Fig. S6, the pure FL dye itself degraded (∼25%) under daylight without catalyst. As clearly seen in Fig. 6d that a bright greenish yellow colored FL dye solution was completely disappeared after 30min of daylight irradiation and a steady decrease in the color was observed in case of N-doped ZnO photocatalyst. This result was compared with Ag/TiO2 photocatalyst, which essentially needed ∼2h of visible light to decompose 83% of FL dye [25]. Also, around 4h of visible light is essential to degrade ∼60% of FL dye by surface modified TiO2 photocatalyst [26]. Therefore, based on the above results, a superior photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO along with the compatibility on three different commercial dyes under daylight irradiation makes us to recommend to be a suitable candidate to treat huge volume of wastewater in industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for superior photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The superior photocatalytic performance of CQD/N-ZnO composite primarily from the combinational effect of both CQD and N-doping characteristics, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The major reasons are: (1) Both CQD and N-doping help to separate the photogenerated electron–hole pairs by hindering the charge recombination to generate photo-reactive sites owing to be act as an electron reservoir [17,24,27]. As soon as the electron–hole pair generates upon supplying of energy higher or equal to the band gap energy of photocatalyst, such energy levels are beneficial for transferring electrons from ZnO conduction band into the closely located charge transfer states of CQDs, which will efficiently separate the photogenerated electron–hole pairs by hindering the charge recombination to generate photo-reactive sites [24]. (2) A stronger electronic coupling of π electrons of carbon with the conduction band of ZnO is applicable, since the work-function of CQDs is placed at −4.5eV, just below the conduction band energy of ZnO is −4.05eV, leading to the up-conversion [6] and to extend the absorption capability into longer wavelength region [6]. Possibly, a large number of photo-reactive sites are created when hole stays near the valence bands of ZnO, which helps to produce highly energetic free radicals. For instance, rich electrons at the CQDs and ZnO can react with adsorbed oxygen molecules to produce superoxide radicals (
O2 −). Simultaneously, the holes migrate to the surface and react with water molecules to produce hydroxide (
OH). Also, upon protonation of superoxide generates the perhydroxide (
OOH). On the whole, these high energy free-radicals are responsible for decomposition of larger molecules into the smaller fragments [28]. Thus, the superior photocatalytic activity of CQD/N-ZnO is mainly due to the combinational effect of N-doping [27,29] and CQD [6–8]. These reactions can be summarized as follows:
(1)
ZnO / CQDs + h ν →
e
-
( CQDs ) +
h
+
( ZnO )
(2)
e
-
+
O
2
→
O
2
-
(3)
h
+
+
OH
-
→
OH
(4)
O
2
-
+
H
2
O →
HO
2
+
OH
-
(5)
HO
2
+
H
2
O →
H
2
O
2
+
OH
(6)
H
2
O
2
→ 2
OH
(7)
OH
+ dye →
CO
2
+
H
2
O
(8)
HO
2
+ dye →
CO
2
+
H
2
O
(9)
O
2
-
+ dye →
CO
2
+
H
2
O

Finally, as far as an industrial application is concerned, the anti-photocorrosion is one of the significant characteristics to be evaluated for reusing the photocatalyst continuously in the wastewater treatment. Hence, the repeated experiments were conducted with these photocatalysts and results indicated that CQD/N-ZnO exhibits an outstanding catalytic stability over N-doped ZnO photocatalyst. The cyclic performance of CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst shows 100%, 93% and 89% of MG dye degradation, in 1st, 2nd and 3rd cycles (60min of daylight irradiation), respectively beyond their weight loses occurred during replication of experiments (Fig. S7). The expected mass loss is lower than 2–3% by weight per cycle. The reduction in the degradation efficiency of CQD/N-ZnO composites probably due to the mass loss percent of photocatalysts during each cycle. A similar trend was observed in other MB and FL dyes, as presented in Fig. S7 and the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra were displayed in Fig. S8.

In addition, the fourth cycle experiment was carried out to investigate the total time required to degrade the entire dyes, as shown in Fig. 7 . In the 4th cycle, CQD/N-ZnO required ∼90min to degrade the dyes completely. For example, it required ∼90, 65 and 85min for MG, MB and FL dyes, respectively. However, N-doped ZnO proceeded with ∼4h for degrading 90% of MG, 95% of MB, and 93% of FL. This observation was strongly confirms the enhanced anti-photocorrosion offered by the CQDs that could effectively inhibits the photocorrosion of ZnO crystals during photocatalytic process. It was confirmed by TEM and XRD measurements of photocatalysts after 4th successive experiments in MG dye. Based on the TEM images, it can be seen clearly that the size and shapes were remain same in CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst. Whereas, wire-like morphologies were resulted from N-doped ZnO (Fig. S8) and the appearance of new XRD peaks at 27.6°, 32.7°, 59.3° and 60.3° representing the degradation of ZnO crystals due to the formation of Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 phase (Fig. S9). Based on these information, it was concluded that the coverage of CQDs could be act as a shield to preserve the ZnO crystals away from the photocorrosion and in addition to the superior photocatalytic performance under naturally available daylight source, thus, it can be recommend as a promising photocatalyst for wastewater treatment in industry.

The CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts were successfully synthesized and characterized to confirm the existence of both CQD and ZnO phases. The significant improvement in the photocatalytic activity of CQD/N-ZnO was noticed in all three commercial dyes under naturally available daylight source. Also, the CQD/N-ZnO photocatalyst possesses an excellent efficiency towards the degradation of dyes and reusability up to 4 times without significant deactivation. The superior photocatalytic activity along with the suppressed photocorrosion are mainly due to the coverage of CQDs that could effectively act as a shield to inhibit the photocorrosion of ZnO crystals. Thus, these kind of photocatalysts were recommended to use as an efficient photocatalyst in industry to remove the various organic dyes under the naturally available daylight, instead of a specific light source.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was encouraged by the Sona College of Technology and supported by National Research Foundation of South Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (2013R1A2A2A07067688, 2010-0019626).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2015.05.046.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: BfR, Collection and pre-selection of available data to be used for the risk assessment of malachite green residues by the Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives, BfR Expert Opinion, vol. 7, 2008, pp. 1–29.
